title: datavis-a2
text:
Identify the gap months (3%)

monthly_dj_check <- monthly_dj |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(
    month = month(date),
    year = year(date),
    months_to_next_row = interval(date, lead(date)) %/% months(1)
  )

# The gap(s): any row where more than 1 month passes before the next entry
gap_start <- monthly_dj_check |>
  filter(months_to_next_row > 1)

gap_start

# A tibble: 1 × 5
  date       close_value month  year months_to_next_row
  <date>           <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>              <dbl>
1 1914-07-30        71.4     7  1914                  5


Running this on your actual file turns up exactly one gap: the row for 30 Jul 1914 jumps straight to 31 Dec 1914 (5 months skipped).
Historical reason (49 words): The New York Stock Exchange closed for over four months (31 Jul–12 Dec 1914) at the outbreak of World War I, fearing foreign investors would dump U.S. securities and drain gold reserves. No trading meant no DJIA prices during that window, creating the gap in the data.

title: datavis-a2
text:
Visualizing DJIA performance by month (10%)
Two new columns, explained simply:

rel_change_pct: “how much did this month’s close change vs last month’s, in percent?” It’s the classic (new - old) / old * 100 formula. We use lag() to grab “last month’s” value.
month_abb: turns the numeric month (1–12) into “Jan”, “Feb”, … and makes it an ordered factor. Without ordering, R would sort months alphabetically (Apr, Aug, Dec…) instead of calendar order — ordering fixes that so your x-axis reads Jan→Dec.


monthly_dj <- monthly_dj |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(
    rel_change_pct = (close_value / lag(close_value) - 1) * 100,
    month_abb = factor(month.abb[month(date)], levels = month.abb, ordered = TRUE)
  )

head(monthly_dj, 10)

# A tibble: 10 × 4
   date       close_value rel_change_pct month_abb
   <date>           <dbl>          <dbl> <ord>    
 1 1896-05-29        40.6         NA     May      
 2 1896-06-30        36.2        -11.0   Jun      
 3 1896-07-31        32.0        -11.4   Jul      
 4 1896-08-31        32.0         -0.156 Aug      
 5 1896-09-30        36.0         12.8   Sep      
 6 1896-10-31        39.5          9.65  Oct      
 7 1896-11-30        41            3.72  Nov      
 8 1896-12-31        40.4         -1.34  Dec      
 9 1897-01-30        42.6          5.22  Jan      
10 1897-02-27        41.7         -2.00  Feb      



Visualization A: violin + box plot, with a line connecting medians
Analogy: a violin plot is like a boxplot that got its shape traced out — instead of just a box, you see the full “silhouette” of how the data is distributed (fat where there’s lots of data, thin where there’s little). We layer a slim boxplot inside the violin (like a box tucked inside its own shadow) by giving it a narrow width, so it stays within the violin’s outline.

monthly_dj_clean <- monthly_dj |> drop_na(rel_change_pct)

medians <- monthly_dj_clean |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarize(median_change = median(rel_change_pct), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(monthly_dj_clean, aes(x = month_abb, y = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "#a6cee3", color = "#1f78b4", trim = TRUE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white") +
  geom_line(data = medians, aes(x = month_abb, y = median_change, group = 1),
            color = "firebrick", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(data = medians, aes(x = month_abb, y = median_change),
             color = "firebrick", size = 1.8) +
  labs(
    title = "DJIA Monthly Relative Change by Calendar Month",
    subtitle = "Violin shows full distribution shape; box shows quartiles; red line tracks the median",
    x = "Month", y = "Relative Change (%)"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = quantile(monthly_dj_clean$rel_change_pct, c(0.01, 0.99))) +
  theme_minimal()








(The coord_cartesian(ylim = ...) line just zooms the y-axis in on the middle 98% of data so a few extreme historical crash months — like Oct 1929 — don’t squash everything else flat. It doesn’t delete any data, it just changes the view, which is important since coord_cartesian — unlike ylim() — never drops points from the stats calculations.)


Visualization B: faceted frequency polygons + rug plot
Analogy: a frequency polygon is just a histogram redrawn as a connected line instead of bars — easier to compare shapes across many small panels. A “rug plot” adds little tick marks along the bottom showing exactly where each individual data point sits, like tick marks on a ruler.
facet_wrap() splits one big plot into a grid of small ones, one per group — here, one panel per month.

means <- monthly_dj_clean |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarize(mean_change = mean(rel_change_pct), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(monthly_dj_clean, aes(x = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_freqpoly(bins = 30, color = "#1f78b4", linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_rug(sides = "b", alpha = 0.3, color = "#1f78b4") +
  geom_vline(data = means, aes(xintercept = mean_change),
             linetype = "dashed", color = "firebrick") +
  facet_wrap(~ month_abb, nrow = 4, ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of DJIA Monthly Relative Change, by Month",
    subtitle = "Dashed red line = mean change for that month",
    x = "Relative Change (%)", y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

text:
Import BTC and gold data (5%)
What tq_transmute() is doing, in plain terms: tq_get() downloads daily price data (like a spreadsheet download from Yahoo Finance). But we want monthly data, so tq_transmute() acts like a “summarizer” — to.monthly collapses each month’s daily prices down to one row per month.

ticker_btc <- "BTC-USD"
ticker_gold <- "GC=F" # Make sure this ticker is correct for Gold
start_date <- "2016-01-01"
end_date <- "2026-06-30"
# BTC Data
monthly_btc <- tq_get(ticker_btc, from = start_date, to = end_date) %>%
  drop_na(adjusted) %>%
  tq_transmute(select = adjusted, mutate_fun = to.monthly, indexAt = "lastof")
# Gold Data
monthly_gold <- tq_get(ticker_gold, from = start_date, to = end_date) %>%
  drop_na(adjusted) %>%
  tq_transmute(select = adjusted, mutate_fun = to.monthly, indexAt = "lastof")

title: datavis-a2
datavis-a2
text:
Volatility analysis: largest monthly drawdown (10%)
What a “drawdown” is, with an analogy: imagine your bank balance over time. Your “peak” is the highest it’s ever been. A “drawdown” at any later point is how far below that peak you’ve fallen, as a percentage. The maximum drawdown is your worst fall from grace — the biggest peak-to-valley drop before it recovered to a new high.
cummax() is the key trick: it walks through the price vector and records “the highest price seen so far” at every point — like a high-water mark on a flood wall. Then drawdown at each point = how far current price sits below that high-water mark.

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  max_drawdown <- abs(min(drawdown_pct))
  max_drawdown
}

# Sanity check: peak of 120 -> trough of 90 is the biggest fall = -25%...
# wait, actually walk through it: 100 -> 60 (peak so far 100, DD -40%)
#                                  60 -> 120 (new peak, DD 0%)
#                                  120 -> 90 (peak 120, DD -25%)
# so the worst drawdown in this vector is -40%, matching the expected answer.
prices_test <- c(100, 60, 120, 90)
compute_drawdown(prices_test)  # should print 40

[1] 40


Now we extend the function slightly so it also tells us when the peak and trough happened — useful for tying the numbers back to real events.

compute_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  trough_idx <- which.min(drawdown_pct)
  peak_idx <- max(which(prices[1:trough_idx] == running_max[trough_idx]))

  tibble(
    max_drawdown_pct = abs(drawdown_pct[trough_idx]),
    peak_date = dates[peak_idx],
    peak_price = prices[peak_idx],
    trough_date = dates[trough_idx],
    trough_price = prices[trough_idx]
  )
}

dj_since_2016 <- monthly_dj |>
  filter(date >= as.Date(start_date)) |>
  arrange(date)

drawdowns_dj <- compute_drawdown_details(dj_since_2016$date, dj_since_2016$close_value)
drawdowns_gold <- compute_drawdown_details(as.Date(monthly_gold$date), monthly_gold$adjusted)
drawdowns_btc <- compute_drawdown_details(as.Date(monthly_btc$date), monthly_btc$adjusted)

biggest_drawdown_dj <- drawdowns_dj$max_drawdown_pct
biggest_drawdown_gold <- drawdowns_gold$max_drawdown_pct
biggest_drawdown_btc <- drawdowns_btc$max_drawdown_pct

Writing your discussion paragraph: once you run the chunk above, look at the printed peak_date / trough_date for each asset and match them to real events, e.g.:

DJIA’s worst 2016–2026 drawdown very likely centers on the Feb–Mar 2020 COVID-19 crash.
Gold’s largest drawdown in this window is a plausible candidate for the 2022 Fed rate-hike cycle (a stronger dollar and higher bond yields make non-yielding gold less attractive).
BTC’s largest drawdown is a plausible candidate for either the Dec 2017–Dec 2018 “crypto winter” or the Nov 2021–Nov 2022 collapse following the Terra/Luna and FTX failures.

Check your actual peak_date/trough_date output against these before writing your final paragraph — don’t state a date you haven’t verified from your own results. A ~100-word paragraph should: (1) state the three drawdown percentages in increasing order, (2) name the peak/trough months per asset, (3) link each to its real-world trigger, and (4) conclude whether the ordering (Gold < DJIA < BTC in volatility) supports the hypothesis.

The source workbook contains five non-data rows before the column headings. The code below skips these rows, retains only the required variables, converts them to the requested classes, removes incomplete observations, and sorts the observations chronologically.

parse_excel_date <- function(x) {
  if (inherits(x, "Date")) {
    return(x)
  }

  if (inherits(x, c("POSIXct", "POSIXt"))) {
    return(as.Date(x))
  }

  mdy(as.character(x))
}

monthly_dj <- read_excel(
  "dja-performance-report-monthly.xlsx",
  skip = 5
) |>
  transmute(
    date = parse_excel_date(`Effective Date`),
    close_value = parse_number(as.character(`Close Value`))
  ) |>
  drop_na(date, close_value) |>
  arrange(date)

# Confirm the required classes
class(monthly_dj$date)

months_to_next_row is calculated from the difference in calendar year and calendar month. This method is preferred to a day-based duration because month-end dates have different numbers of days.

gap_months <- monthly_dj |>
  mutate(
    month = month(date),
    year = year(date),
    months_to_next_row =
      (year(lead(date)) - year(date)) * 12 +
      (month(lead(date)) - month(date))
  ) |>
  filter(months_to_next_row > 1) |>
  select(date, close_value, month, year, months_to_next_row)

gap_months |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Starting Point of Missing DJIA Months**"),
    subtitle = "A gap is flagged when the next observation is more than one calendar month later"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    date = "Date",
    close_value = "DJIA Close",
    month = "Month",
    year = "Year",
    months_to_next_row = "Months to Next Row"
  ) |>
  fmt_date(columns = date, date_style = 3) |>
  fmt_number(columns = close_value, decimals = 2, use_seps = TRUE) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  tab_source_note(md("Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."))

The gap begins after July 1914. The NYSE closed on 31 July when World War I created fears that European investors would liquidate US securities and drain American gold reserves. Stock trading resumed in December 1914, explaining the missing August-November observations.
Source: Silber (2005), Journal of Financial Economics.



The relative monthly change is calculated as the percentage change from the previous monthly close. Month abbreviations are stored as an ordered factor so that all visualisations follow the calendar sequence from January to December.

monthly_dj <- monthly_dj |>
  mutate(
    rel_change_pct = (close_value / lag(close_value) - 1) * 100,
    month_abb = factor(
      month(date),
      levels = 1:12,
      labels = month.abb,
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  )

monthly_statistics <- monthly_dj |>
  drop_na(rel_change_pct) |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarise(
    median_rel_change = median(rel_change_pct),
    mean_rel_change = mean(rel_change_pct),
    .groups = "drop"
  )




The violin plots show the complete distribution for each calendar month. Narrow box plots are placed inside the violin envelopes, while the connected points show how the median monthly return changes across the calendar year.

ggplot(monthly_dj, aes(x = month_abb, y = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = 0,
    colour = "grey55",
    linewidth = 0.5,
    linetype = "dotted"
  ) +
  geom_violin(
    trim = TRUE,
    scale = "width",
    fill = "#9ECAE1",
    colour = "#08519C",
    alpha = 0.75,
    linewidth = 0.6,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.13,
    outlier.shape = NA,
    fill = "white",
    colour = "#08306B",
    alpha = 0.9,
    linewidth = 0.55,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_line(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(
      x = month_abb,
      y = median_rel_change,
      group = 1
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    colour = "#D94801",
    linewidth = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_point(
    data = monthly_statistics,
    aes(
      x = month_abb,
      y = median_rel_change
    ),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    shape = 21,
    size = 2.8,
    stroke = 0.7,
    fill = "#FDD0A2",
    colour = "#D94801"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = "%", accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of DJIA Monthly Returns by Calendar Month",
    subtitle = "Box plots summarise the distributions; the orange line connects monthly medians",
    x = "Calendar month",
    y = "Relative monthly change",
    caption = "Source: DJIA monthly performance workbook supplied for the assessment."
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey40")
  )











Each facet uses the same scales to support direct comparison. The dashed vertical line represents that month’s mean return, while the rug marks display the individual historical observations.

ggplot(monthly_dj, aes(x = rel_change_pct)) +
  geom_freqpoly(
    bins = 24,
    colour = "#2C7FB8",
    linewidth = 0.75,
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_rug(
    sides = "b",

    colour = "#253494",

ticker_btc <- "BTC-USD"

start_date <- "2016-01-01"
end_date <- "2026-06-30"

# Convert Bitcoin daily observations to monthly adjusted prices
monthly_btc <- tq_get(
  ticker_btc,
  from = start_date,

The function returns the percentage decline from the running peak at every observation. A value of zero indicates a new high; a larger value indicates a deeper fall below the previous peak.

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  if (!is.numeric(prices)) {
    stop("prices must be a numeric vector")
  }

  if (anyNA(prices)) {
    stop("prices must not contain missing values")
  }

  if (any(prices <= 0)) {
    stop("prices must be strictly positive")
  }

  running_peak <- cummax(prices)
  (running_peak - prices) / running_peak * 100
}

The helper function below identifies the peak that existed before the worst trough, ensuring that the reported peak always occurs before its corresponding trough.

get_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices, asset_name) {
  drawdown_pct <- compute_drawdown(prices)
  trough_index <- which.max(drawdown_pct)
  peak_index <- which.max(prices[seq_len(trough_index)])

  tibble(
    asset = asset_name,
    peak_month = floor_date(dates[peak_index], unit = "month"),
    peak_value = prices[peak_index],
    trough_month = floor_date(dates[trough_index], unit = "month"),
    trough_value = prices[trough_index],
    largest_drawdown_pct = drawdown_pct[trough_index]
  )
}

drawdown_summary <- bind_rows(
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_gold$date,
    monthly_gold$adjusted,
    "Gold"
  ),
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_dj_decade$date,
    monthly_dj_decade$close_value,
    "DJIA"
  ),
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_btc$date,
    monthly_btc$adjusted,
    "Bitcoin"
  )
) |>
  mutate(
    asset = factor(asset, levels = c("Gold", "DJIA", "Bitcoin"))
  ) |>
  arrange(asset)

drawdown_summary_display <- drawdown_summary |>
  mutate(
    peak_month = format(peak_month, "%b %Y"),
    trough_month = format(trough_month, "%b %Y")
  )

drawdown_summary_display |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Largest Monthly Peak-to-Trough Drawdowns**"),
    subtitle = "January 2016 to the latest common month"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    asset = "Asset",
    peak_month = "Peak Month",
    peak_value = "Peak Price",
    trough_month = "Trough Month",
    trough_value = "Trough Price",
    largest_drawdown_pct = "Largest Drawdown"
  ) |>
  fmt_currency(
    columns = c(peak_value, trough_value),
    currency = "USD",
    decimals = 2
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = largest_drawdown_pct,
    decimals = 1,
    pattern = "{x}%"
  ) |>
  cols_align(align = "center", columns = everything()) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  opt_row_striping() |>
  tab_source_note(
    md("Drawdown = percentage fall from the running peak to a subsequent monthly trough.")
  )

Gold recorded the smallest maximum drawdown (23.1%) from Feb 2026 to Jun 2026, moving from safe-haven demand to pressure from higher yields and a stronger US dollar. The DJIA fell 23.2% from Dec 2019 to Mar 2020 during the COVID-19 market shock. Bitcoin dropped 75.6% from Dec 2017 to Jan 2019 as a speculative boom reversed into a crypto winter. The ordering Gold < DJIA < Bitcoin supports the hypothesis, although drawdown measures only the single worst historical decline.

The source workbook contains five non-data rows before the column headings. The code below skips these rows, retains only the required variables, converts them to the requested classes, removes incomplete observations, and sorts the observations chronologically.

parse_excel_date <- function(x) {
  if (inherits(x, "Date")) {
    return(x)
  }

  if (inherits(x, c("POSIXct", "POSIXt"))) {
    return(as.Date(x))
  }

  mdy(as.character(x))
}

monthly_dj <- read_excel(
  "dja-performance-report-monthly.xlsx",
  skip = 5
) |>
  transmute(
    date = parse_excel_date(`Effective Date`),
    close_value = parse_number(as.character(`Close Value`))
  ) |>
  drop_na(date, close_value) |>
  arrange(date)

The relative monthly change is calculated as the percentage change from the previous monthly close. Month abbreviations are stored as an ordered factor so that all visualisations follow the calendar sequence from January to December.

monthly_dj <- monthly_dj |>
  mutate(
    rel_change_pct = (close_value / lag(close_value) - 1) * 100,
    month_abb = factor(
      month(date),
      levels = 1:12,
      labels = month.abb,
      ordered = TRUE
    )
  )

monthly_statistics <- monthly_dj |>
  drop_na(rel_change_pct) |>
  group_by(month_abb) |>
  summarise(
    median_rel_change = median(rel_change_pct),
    mean_rel_change = mean(rel_change_pct),
    .groups = "drop"
  )




The violin plots show the complete distribution for each calendar month. Narrow box plots are placed inside the violin envelopes, while the connected points show how the median monthly return changes across the calendar year.

The function returns the percentage decline from the running peak at every observation. A value of zero indicates a new high; a larger value indicates a deeper fall below the previous peak.

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  if (!is.numeric(prices)) {
    stop("prices must be a numeric vector")
  }

  if (anyNA(prices)) {
    stop("prices must not contain missing values")
  }

  if (any(prices <= 0)) {
    stop("prices must be strictly positive")
  }

  running_peak <- cummax(prices)
  (running_peak - prices) / running_peak * 100
}

The helper function below identifies the peak that existed before the worst trough, ensuring that the reported peak always occurs before its corresponding trough.

get_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices, asset_name) {
  drawdown_pct <- compute_drawdown(prices)
  trough_index <- which.max(drawdown_pct)
  peak_index <- which.max(prices[seq_len(trough_index)])

  tibble(
    asset = asset_name,
    peak_month = floor_date(dates[peak_index], unit = "month"),
    peak_value = prices[peak_index],
    trough_month = floor_date(dates[trough_index], unit = "month"),
    trough_value = prices[trough_index],
    largest_drawdown_pct = drawdown_pct[trough_index]
  )
}

drawdown_summary <- bind_rows(
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_gold$date,
    monthly_gold$adjusted,
    "Gold"

  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_dj_decade$date,
    monthly_dj_decade$close_value,
    "DJIA"
  ),
  get_drawdown_details(
    monthly_btc$date,
    monthly_btc$adjusted,
    "Bitcoin"
  )
) |>

Historical reason: The New York Stock Exchange closed for over four months (31 Jul–12 Dec 1914) at the outbreak of World War I, fearing foreign investors would dump U.S. securities and drain gold reserves. No trading meant no DJIA prices during that window, creating the gap in the data.

text:
Volatility analysis: largest monthly drawdown (10%)

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  max_drawdown <- abs(min(drawdown_pct))
  max_drawdown
}

# Sanity check: peak of 120 -> trough of 90 is the biggest fall = -25%...
# wait, actually walk through it: 100 -> 60 (peak so far 100, DD -40%)
#                                  60 -> 120 (new peak, DD 0%)
#                                  120 -> 90 (peak 120, DD -25%)
# so the worst drawdown in this vector is -40%, matching the expected answer.
prices_test <- c(100, 60, 120, 90)
compute_drawdown(prices_test)  # should print 40

[1] 40


Now we extend the function slightly so it also tells us when the peak and trough happened — useful for tying the numbers back to real events.

compute_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  trough_idx <- which.min(drawdown_pct)
  peak_idx <- max(which(prices[1:trough_idx] == running_max[trough_idx]))

  tibble(
    max_drawdown_pct = abs(drawdown_pct[trough_idx]),
    peak_date = dates[peak_idx],
    peak_price = prices[peak_idx],
    trough_date = dates[trough_idx],
    trough_price = prices[trough_idx]
  )
}

  filter(date >= as.Date(start_date)) |>

drawdowns_btc <- compute_drawdown_details(as.Date(monthly_btc$date), monthly_btc$adjusted)

Discussion
Gold recorded the smallest maximum drawdown (23.1%), falling from its peak in February 2026 to its trough in June 2026, indicating relatively stable price movements despite market uncertainty. The DJIA experienced a slightly larger drawdown (23.2%) between December 2019 and March 2020, corresponding to the global market crash triggered by the COVID-19 pandemic. Bitcoin suffered the largest drawdown (75.6%) from December 2017 to January 2019 during the cryptocurrency market downturn following the 2017 speculative bubble. Overall, the results support the hypothesis that volatility increases from Gold to DJIA and finally Bitcoin.

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
2.1 Introduction
This report analyses the English Premier League match results using R, the tidyverse suite and the gt package. The first part generates the final league table based on total points, goal difference and goals scored. The second part proposes a Home Advantage Index (HAI) to quantify how much each team benefits from playing at home.
The analysis uses match-level results from all 20 Premier League teams and presents the findings through ranked and professionally formatted tables.

results <- read_csv("epl_results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
teams <- read_csv("epl_teams.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
3.1 Generating the League Table

3.1.1 Transforming the Match Results
The original dataset stores match results in a wide format, where each away team is represented as a separate column. To simplify the analysis, the data is reshaped into a long format so that each row represents a single match between a home team and an away team.

matches <- results |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -home_team,
    names_to = "away_team",
    values_to = "result"
  ) |>
  filter(result != "-")

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
3.2 Separating Home and Away Goals
Match results are initially stored as text in the format home goals–away goals (e.g., “3-2”). This step separates the score into two numerical columns, allowing the goals scored by each team to be analysed and compared.

matches <- matches |>
  separate_wider_delim(
    result,
    delim = "-",
    names = c("home_goals", "away_goals")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    home_goals = as.integer(home_goals),
    away_goals = as.integer(away_goals)
  )

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
3.3 Calculating Team Results
Each match is converted into two team-level records: one for the home team and one for the away team. Points are then awarded according to Premier League rules, where a win earns three points, a draw earns one point and a loss earns zero points.

home_records <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = home_team,
    goals_scored = home_goals,
    goals_conceded = away_goals,
    points = case_when(
      home_goals > away_goals ~ 3,
      home_goals == away_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )

away_records <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = away_team,
    goals_scored = away_goals,
    goals_conceded = home_goals,
    points = case_when(
      away_goals > home_goals ~ 3,
      away_goals == home_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )


team_records <- bind_rows(home_records, away_records)

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
3.4 Summarizing the League Results
The individual match records are aggregated to calculate each team’s season statistics, including total points, goals scored, goals conceded and goal difference. These values are then used to rank the teams according to the official Premier League standings.

league_table <- team_records |>
  mutate(
    win = if_else(points == 3, 1, 0),
    draw = if_else(points == 1, 1, 0),
    loss = if_else(points == 0, 1, 0)
  ) |>
  group_by(team) |>
  summarise(
    MP = n(),
    W = sum(win),
    D = sum(draw),
    L = sum(loss),
    GF = sum(goals_scored),
    GA = sum(goals_conceded),

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
4.1 Metric Definition
The Home Advantage Index measures how much better a team performs at home compared with away. It is calculated by subtracting away points per game from home points per game. A higher positive value indicates a stronger home advantage, while a value close to zero suggests similar performance at home and away. A negative value means the team earns more points per game away from home.

home_performance <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = home_team,
    venue = "Home",
    goals_scored = home_goals,
    goals_conceded = away_goals,
    points = case_when(
      home_goals > away_goals ~ 3,
      home_goals == away_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )

away_performance <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = away_team,
    venue = "Away",
    goals_scored = away_goals,
    goals_conceded = home_goals,
    points = case_when(
      away_goals > home_goals ~ 3,
      away_goals == home_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )

venue_performance <- bind_rows(
  home_performance,
  away_performance
)

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
4.2 Calculating the Home Advantage Index

To calculate the Home Advantage Index (HAI), each team’s home and away performances are analysed separately. The average points earned per game at home and away are calculated, and the difference between these two values is used as the HAI. A larger positive value indicates that a team performs significantly better when playing at home.


home_advantage_table <- venue_performance |>
  group_by(team, venue) |>
  summarise(
    games_played = n(),
    total_points = sum(points),
    points_per_game = total_points / games_played,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  select(
    team,
    venue,
    points_per_game
  ) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = venue,
    values_from = points_per_game
  ) |>
  mutate(
    metric_value = Home - Away
  ) |>
  left_join(
    teams,
    by = c("team" = "abbr")
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(metric_value)) |>
  mutate(
    metric_rank = row_number()
  ) |>
  transmute(
  metric_rank,
  team = name,
  metric_value = round(metric_value, 2),
  home_ppg = round(Home, 2),
  away_ppg = round(Away, 2)
  )

The proposed Home Advantage Index provides a meaningful and interpretable measure of home advantage by comparing points earned per game at home and away. Unlike total points, points per game normalises performance across an equal number of fixtures, ensuring fair comparisons between teams. The results indicate that Tottenham Hotspur exhibits the strongest home advantage, while clubs with lower HAI values perform more consistently across venues. Using points per game rather than total points ensures the comparison remains fair and independent of league position, making the metric suitable for comparing teams with different overall strengths.

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
5.3 Identify the gap months (3%)
Historical reason: The New York Stock Exchange closed for over four months (31 Jul–12 Dec 1914) at the outbreak of World War I, fearing foreign investors would dump U.S. securities and drain gold reserves. No trading meant no DJIA prices during that window, creating the gap in the data.

monthly_dj_check <- monthly_dj |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(
    month = month(date),
    year = year(date),
    months_to_next_row = interval(date, lead(date)) %/% months(1)
  )

# The gap(s): any row where more than 1 month passes before the next entry
gap_start <- monthly_dj_check |>
  filter(months_to_next_row > 1)

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
6.3 Volatility analysis: largest monthly drawdown (10%)

compute_drawdown <- function(prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  max_drawdown <- abs(min(drawdown_pct))
  max_drawdown
}

# Sanity check: peak of 120 -> trough of 90 is the biggest fall = -25%...
# wait, actually walk through it: 100 -> 60 (peak so far 100, DD -40%)
#                                  60 -> 120 (new peak, DD 0%)
#                                  120 -> 90 (peak 120, DD -25%)
# so the worst drawdown in this vector is -40%, matching the expected answer.
prices_test <- c(100, 60, 120, 90)
compute_drawdown(prices_test)  # should print 40

[1] 40


Now we extend the function slightly so it also tells us when the peak and trough happened — useful for tying the numbers back to real events.

compute_drawdown_details <- function(dates, prices) {
  running_max <- cummax(prices)
  drawdown_pct <- (prices - running_max) / running_max * 100
  trough_idx <- which.min(drawdown_pct)
  peak_idx <- max(which(prices[1:trough_idx] == running_max[trough_idx]))

  tibble(
    max_drawdown_pct = abs(drawdown_pct[trough_idx]),
    peak_date = dates[peak_idx],
    peak_price = prices[peak_idx],
    trough_date = dates[trough_idx],
    trough_price = prices[trough_idx]
  )
}

dj_since_2016 <- monthly_dj |>
  filter(date >= as.Date(start_date)) |>
  arrange(date)

drawdowns_dj <- compute_drawdown_details(dj_since_2016$date, dj_since_2016$close_value)
drawdowns_gold <- compute_drawdown_details(as.Date(monthly_gold$date), monthly_gold$adjusted)

text:
6.4 Conclusion
Gold recorded the smallest maximum drawdown (23.1%), falling from its peak in February 2026 to its trough in June 2026, indicating relatively stable price movements despite market uncertainty. The DJIA experienced a slightly larger drawdown (23.2%) between December 2019 and March 2020, corresponding to the global market crash triggered by the COVID-19 pandemic. Bitcoin suffered the largest drawdown (75.6%) from December 2017 to January 2019 during the cryptocurrency market downturn following the 2017 speculative bubble. Overall, the results support the hypothesis that volatility increases from Gold to DJIA and finally Bitcoin.

I mainly used ChatGPT throughout this assessment. Rather than using it to complete the assignment for me, I treated it as a learning companion whenever I was stuck or unsure about how to approach a problem.

text:
8.2 How did the tool help you? Provide concrete examples.
ChatGPT was especially useful when I encountered coding errors or was unsure how to structure my analysis. It helped me understand how to transform the EPL dataset from a wide format into a long format using pivot_longer(), split the match results into home and away goals, and calculate league statistics such as points, goal difference and goals scored. When I ran into errors while rendering my Quarto report or formatting the gt tables, I could describe the problem or share the error message, and it helped me identify what went wrong much faster than searching through documentation. Beyond coding, it also gave suggestions on how to improve the report layout and helped me write short explanations for each section so that the report flowed more naturally.

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
section: 8.3 What do you think is challenging about the tool?
text:
8.3 What do you think is challenging about the tool?
One thing I realised is that ChatGPT is only as helpful as the prompts I give it. If my question was too vague, the answers were often too generic or didn’t solve my actual problem. I frequently had to rephrase my questions, provide screenshots, or explain exactly what I wanted before getting a useful response.
I also found that I couldn’t simply accept every suggestion without thinking. For example, when designing the Home Advantage Index, ChatGPT could suggest different ways to measure home advantage, but I still had to decide which metric made the most sense for the assignment and justify why I chose it. The same applied to the final Premier League table—I still had to think about what statistics should be included, how they should be presented, and whether the output matched the assignment requirements. It reminded me that AI can support the thinking process, but it cannot replace my own judgement.

Overall, I found ChatGPT to be a really helpful learning tool. Instead of spending a long time searching through forums or documentation, I could ask questions and get explanations that were tailored to the problem I was facing. That made debugging much less frustrating and allowed me to spend more time understanding the concepts instead of getting stuck on small syntax issues.

title: English Premier League Match Analysis
text:
This report analyses the English Premier League match results using R, the tidyverse suite and the gt package. The first part generates the final league table based on total points, goal difference and goals scored. The second part proposes a Home Advantage Index (HAI) to quantify how much each team benefits from playing at home.
The analysis uses match-level results from all 20 Premier League teams and presents the findings through ranked and professionally formatted tables.

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

results <- read_csv("epl_results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
teams <- read_csv("epl_teams.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

title: English Premier League Match Analysis
text:
1.1 Generating the League Table

1.1.1 Transforming the Match Results
The original dataset stores match results in a wide format, where each away team is represented as a separate column. To simplify the analysis, the data is reshaped into a long format so that each row represents a single match between a home team and an away team.

matches <- results |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -home_team,
    names_to = "away_team",
    values_to = "result"
  ) |>
  filter(result != "-")

title: English Premier League Match Analysis
text:
1.2 Separating Home and Away Goals
Match results are initially stored as text in the format home goals–away goals (e.g., “3-2”). This step separates the score into two numerical columns, allowing the goals scored by each team to be analysed and compared.

matches <- matches |>
  separate_wider_delim(
    result,
    delim = "-",
    names = c("home_goals", "away_goals")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    home_goals = as.integer(home_goals),
    away_goals = as.integer(away_goals)
  )

title: English Premier League Match Analysis
text:
1.3 Calculating Team Results
Each match is converted into two team-level records: one for the home team and one for the away team. Points are then awarded according to Premier League rules, where a win earns three points, a draw earns one point and a loss earns zero points.

home_records <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = home_team,
    goals_scored = home_goals,
    goals_conceded = away_goals,
    points = case_when(
      home_goals > away_goals ~ 3,
      home_goals == away_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )

away_records <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = away_team,
    goals_scored = away_goals,
    goals_conceded = home_goals,
    points = case_when(
      away_goals > home_goals ~ 3,
      away_goals == home_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )


team_records <- bind_rows(home_records, away_records)

title: English Premier League Match Analysis
text:
1.4 Summarizing the League Results
The individual match records are aggregated to calculate each team’s season statistics, including total points, goals scored, goals conceded and goal difference. These values are then used to rank the teams according to the official Premier League standings.

league_table <- team_records |>
  mutate(
    win = if_else(points == 3, 1, 0),
    draw = if_else(points == 1, 1, 0),
    loss = if_else(points == 0, 1, 0)
  ) |>
  group_by(team) |>
  summarise(
    MP = n(),
    W = sum(win),
    D = sum(draw),
    L = sum(loss),
    GF = sum(goals_scored),
    GA = sum(goals_conceded),
    GD = GF - GA,
    Pts = sum(points),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(
    teams,
    by = c("team" = "abbr")
  ) |>
  select(
    Team = name,
    MP,
    W,
    D,
    L,
    GF,
    GA,
    GD,
    Pts
  ) |>
  arrange(
    desc(Pts),
    desc(GD),
    desc(GF)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Pos = row_number()
  ) |>
  select(
    Pos,
    Team,
    MP,
    W,
    D,
    L,
    GF,
    GA,
    GD,
    Pts
  )

title: English Premier League Match Analysis
text:
2.1 Metric Definition
The Home Advantage Index measures how much better a team performs at home compared with away. It is calculated by subtracting away points per game from home points per game. A higher positive value indicates a stronger home advantage, while a value close to zero suggests similar performance at home and away. A negative value means the team earns more points per game away from home.

home_performance <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = home_team,
    venue = "Home",
    goals_scored = home_goals,
    goals_conceded = away_goals,
    points = case_when(
      home_goals > away_goals ~ 3,
      home_goals == away_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )

away_performance <- matches |>
  transmute(
    team = away_team,
    venue = "Away",
    goals_scored = away_goals,
    goals_conceded = home_goals,
    points = case_when(
      away_goals > home_goals ~ 3,
      away_goals == home_goals ~ 1,
      TRUE ~ 0
    )
  )

venue_performance <- bind_rows(
  home_performance,
  away_performance
)

title: English Premier League Match Analysis
text:
2.2 Calculating the Home Advantage Index

To calculate the Home Advantage Index (HAI), each team’s home and away performances are analysed separately. The average points earned per game at home and away are calculated, and the difference between these two values is used as the HAI. A larger positive value indicates that a team performs significantly better when playing at home.


home_advantage_table <- venue_performance |>
  group_by(team, venue) |>
  summarise(
    games_played = n(),
    total_points = sum(points),
    points_per_game = total_points / games_played,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  select(
    team,
    venue,
    points_per_game
  ) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = venue,
    values_from = points_per_game
  ) |>
  mutate(
    metric_value = Home - Away
  ) |>
  left_join(
    teams,
    by = c("team" = "abbr")
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(metric_value)) |>
  mutate(
    metric_rank = row_number()
  ) |>
  transmute(
  metric_rank,
  team = name,
  metric_value = round(metric_value, 2),
  home_ppg = round(Home, 2),
  away_ppg = round(Away, 2)
  )

The proposed Home Advantage Index provides a meaningful and interpretable measure of home advantage by comparing points earned per game at home and away. Unlike total points, points per game normalises performance across an equal number of fixtures, ensuring fair comparisons between teams. The results indicate that Tottenham Hotspur exhibits the strongest home advantage, while clubs with lower HAI values perform more consistently across venues. Using points per game rather than total points ensures the comparison remains fair and independent of league position, making the metric suitable for comparing teams with different overall strengths.

    'Food & Beverage'  = "#E68A1E",
    'Accommodation'  = "#D64545"
  )) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = ifelse(value/1000 >= 2.5, sprintf("%.3f", value/1000), "")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    size = 2,
    color = "black"
  ) +
  guides(fill = guide_legend(nrow = 3, ncol = 2, byrow = FALSE)) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_blank(),
        legend.key.size = unit(0.25, "cm"),
        plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#FDF3DE", color = NA),
        panel.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
        ) +
  labs(
    x = "Amount (Billion SGD)",
    y = "Quarter",
    title = "Singapore 2019-2024 Quarterly Tourism Receipts By Major Components",
    caption = 'Source: singstat.gov.sg'
  ) + 
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 10, ymin = 4, ymax = 14, alpha = .25) +
  annotate("text", x = 5, y = 10, label = "COVID-19 Pandemic") + 
  annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 30, ymin = 15, ymax = 25, alpha = .25) +
  annotate("text", x = 20, y = 21, label = "Tourism restrictions ease")
p

title: Data Engineering and Visualisation Assessment 3
text:
7.3 Data Interpretation
During the COVID-19 pandemic, tourism numbers collapsed: 2021-Q1 shows the lowest total receipt (far below S$1 billion, versus S$8.026 billion for “Other Components” alone in 2019-Q4), reflecting Singapore’s border closures.
Recovery was rapid once the pandemic subsided and borders reopened. 2023-Q4 saw a total receipt of S$27.155 billion, nearly matching pre-pandemic levels (S$29.192 billion in 2019-Q4).
Across all quarters, “Other Components” and “Sightseeing, Entertainment & Gaming” consistently form the largest shares, while “Food & Beverage” remains the smallest contributor (e.g. S$3.192 billion in 2023-Q3 versus S$6.866 billion for “Other Components” that same quarter).
